topics.name: Types of Software Applications and Web Basics
subtopics: Types of software applications — Desktop, Web, Mobile, Distributed, AI, IoT
Full Stack career — Frontend, Backend, Database roles
Client-Server Architecture — how request travels from browser to server
Fundamentals of Internet — IP Address, SMTP, FTP, HTTP, Request Format
Web vs Website vs Web Application distinction
required_content: Desktop apps run on OS directly (Photoshop, MS Word). Web apps run in browser, no installation needed (Gmail, YouTube)
Full Stack = Frontend (HTML/CSS/JS) + Backend (Node.js/Python) + Database (SQL/MongoDB)
Client sends HTTP request → DNS resolves domain → Server processes → sends HTTP response → Browser renders
IP Address: unique identifier for every device on internet. IPv4 (192.168.1.1), IPv6 (longer format)
HTTP methods: GET (fetch data), POST (send data). Status codes: 200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error
required_diagrams: Client-Server request-response flow: Browser → DNS Server → Web Server → Database → Response back to Browser. Show each step labeled with protocol names (HTTP, DNS lookup, SQL query).
required_tables: Comparison: App Type | Runs On | Installation | Examples || Desktop | OS directly | Required | Photoshop, VLC || Web App | Browser | Not required | Gmail, YouTube || Mobile | Mobile OS | Required | Instagram, WhatsApp || Distributed | Multiple servers | N/A | Google Search, Netflix
common_mistakes: Thinking website and web application are the same — a website is static (blog), a web app is dynamic (Gmail)
Confusing frontend and backend — frontend is what user sees, backend is server logic user never sees

topics.name: Setting Up the Development Environment
subtopics: VS Code installation and essential extensions for web development
Node.js and npm — what they are and why frontend developers need them
Folder structure for a web project
Different stages — local development, web server, deployment
required_content: VS Code extensions: Live Server (auto-refresh), Prettier (auto-format), ESLint (error detection), HTML CSS Support
Node.js is a JS runtime. npm (Node Package Manager) installs libraries like Bootstrap, React
Standard folder: index.html at root, /css folder, /js folder, /images folder
Development → Testing → Staging → Production pipeline
topics.required_code:
/* Folder structure for web project */
project/
├── index.html
├── css/
│   └── style.css
├── js/
│   └── app.js
└── images/
    └── logo.png
common_mistakes: Putting all files in root folder — becomes unmanageable as project grows
Thinking Node.js is only for backend — frontend devs use it daily for npm packages

topics.name: Browser Architecture and DOM Overview
subtopics: Browser components — Browser Engine, JS Engine, Rendering Engine, Networking
Programming language vs Markup language distinction
HTML parsing process — how browser reads HTML and builds DOM
DOM Overview — what is the Document Object Model
required_content: Browser components: Browser Engine (coordinates all), Rendering Engine (paints pixels — Blink in Chrome), JS Engine (executes JS — V8 in Chrome), Networking (handles HTTP requests)
Markup language (HTML) describes structure/content. Programming language (JS) has logic, loops, conditions
HTML parsing: browser reads HTML top to bottom → builds DOM tree → combines with CSSOM → creates Render Tree → paints to screen
DOM = Document Object Model. Tree structure where every HTML element is a node. JS can read/modify any node
required_diagrams: Browser architecture diagram: show 5 boxes — Browser Engine at center connecting to Rendering Engine, JS Engine (V8), Networking layer, and Data Persistence. Show HTML/CSS going into Rendering Engine, JS going into JS Engine.
DOM tree diagram: show HTML document as a tree. Root = document node, children = html, then head/body, then title/h1/p etc. Each element is a labeled node.
common_mistakes: Thinking HTML is a programming language — it has no logic, no conditions, no loops
Thinking DOM and HTML file are the same — DOM is the browser's live memory representation, HTML is just the source file

topics.name: HTML Document Structure and Basic Tags
subtopics: Writing first HTML program — Hello World
Document structure — DOCTYPE, html, head, body
Head section — title, meta tags, link, style, script
Meta tags — charset, viewport, keywords, description, refresh
required_content: Every HTML file must start with <!DOCTYPE html> — tells browser this is HTML5
head section: metadata not shown to user (title, meta, css links, js links)
body section: everything visible on page (headings, paragraphs, images, forms)
meta charset='UTF-8' — supports all languages including Hindi, Chinese
meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width' — essential for mobile responsiveness
topics.required_code:
Hello, World!

This is my first webpage.
common_mistakes: Forgetting DOCTYPE — causes browser to enter 'quirks mode' and render incorrectly
Putting visible content inside head — it won't show on page
Missing closing tags — causes unpredictable rendering

topics.name: HTML Elements, Attributes and Colors
subtopics: Types of HTML elements — Normal, Void, Raw Text, RC Data, Foreign
HTML attributes — id, class, style, src, href, alt
Defining colors — color name, hexadecimal, RGB, RGBA, HSL, HSLA
Relative vs absolute paths for links and images
required_content:
Normal elements: have opening and closing tags (

,
). Void elements: self-closing, no content (,
, ,
)
id: unique identifier for one element. class: reusable identifier for multiple elements. id used for JS targeting, class used for CSS styling
Color formats: name ('red'), hex (#FF0000 or #F00), rgb(255,0,0), rgba(255,0,0,0.5) — a=opacity, hsl(0,100%,50%)
Absolute path: full URL (https://site.com/img.jpg). Relative path: from current file (../images/img.jpg)
topics.required_code:
Color name

Hex color

RGB color

RGBA with 70% opacity

HSL color
required_tables: Color formats: Format | Syntax | Supports Opacity | Example || Name | red | No | color: red || Hex | #RRGGBB | No (#RRGGBBAA yes) | #FF5733 || RGB | rgb(r,g,b) | No | rgb(255,87,51) || RGBA | rgba(r,g,b,a) | Yes (0-1) | rgba(255,87,51,0.5) || HSL | hsl(h,s%,l%) | No | hsl(14,100%,60%) || HSLA | hsla(h,s%,l%,a) | Yes | hsla(14,100%,60%,0.5)
common_mistakes: Using id for multiple elements — id must be unique per page, use class for multiple elements
Confusing rgba alpha (0=transparent, 1=opaque) with CSS opacity (same range but different scope)

topics.name: Semantic HTML and Layout Elements
subtopics: Semantic elements — header, nav, main, article, section, aside, footer
Non-semantic elements — div and span
When to use semantic vs non-semantic elements
DOM structure and parent-child-sibling relationships
required_content: Semantic elements tell browser AND developer what content is inside. <header> = top section, <nav> = navigation links, <main> = main content, <footer> = bottom section
div = block-level container with no meaning. span = inline container with no meaning. Used when no semantic element fits
Benefits of semantic HTML: better SEO (Google understands content), screen reader accessibility, cleaner code
DOM relationships: parent contains children. Siblings share same parent. CSS can target based on these relationships
topics.required_code:
Home About

Article Title

Article content...

Related Links

Copyright 2024
required_diagrams: Semantic HTML page layout diagram: show a webpage wireframe divided into labeled sections — header (top), nav (inside header), main (center, containing article + aside side by side), footer (bottom). Each section labeled with its HTML tag name.
required_tables: Comparison: Element | Type | Meaning | Use when || <div> | Non-semantic | None | Generic grouping, no meaning || <header> | Semantic | Page/section top | Site header, article header || <nav> | Semantic | Navigation | Menu, breadcrumbs || <main> | Semantic | Primary content | Main page content (once per page) || <article> | Semantic | Self-contained content | Blog post, news article || <aside> | Semantic | Tangentially related | Sidebar, callout box || <footer> | Semantic | Bottom section | Copyright, links, contact
common_mistakes: Using <div> for everything — loses semantic meaning, hurts SEO and accessibility
Using <header> tag inside <header> tag — valid but confusing, use <div> for inner grouping

topics.name: Text Formatting, Lists and Tables
subtopics: Text formatting tags — b, i, em, strong, u, ins, strike, del, sup, sub
Ordered and Unordered Lists — type, start, reversed, nesting
HTML Tables — table, thead, tbody, tfoot, tr, td, th, caption, colgroup
Table attributes — border, cellspacing, cellpadding, colspan, rowspan
required_content: <strong> = semantic bold (important content). <b> = visual bold only. <em> = semantic italic (emphasis). <i> = visual italic. Use semantic versions for accessibility
Ordered list: <ol type='A'> for A,B,C. type='i' for i,ii,iii. start='5' begins from 5. reversed reverses order
Table structure: <table> → <thead> (header rows) → <tbody> (data rows) → <tfoot> (summary row). <th> for header cells, <td> for data cells
colspan='2' makes cell span 2 columns. rowspan='3' makes cell span 3 rows. Used for merged cells in complex tables
topics.required_code:
Student Results
NameScoreGrade
Alice92A
Bob75B
Average83.5
common_mistakes: Forgetting <thead>/<tbody> — technically works but bad practice, breaks CSS nth-child targeting
Using tables for page layout — tables are for tabular DATA only, use flexbox/grid for layout

topics.name: Images and Links
subtopics: Images — img tag, src, alt, width, height, srcset for responsive images
Links — anchor tag, href, target, download attribute, intra vs inter document links
URL vs URI distinction
iframe — embedding external pages, YouTube videos
required_content: img src='path' alt='description'. alt is mandatory for accessibility — screen readers read it. width/height in px or %
target='_blank' opens link in new tab. target='_self' (default) opens in same tab. Intra-document: href='#section-id'
srcset='small.jpg 480w, large.jpg 1080w' — browser picks right image based on screen width. Saves bandwidth on mobile
iframe src='https://youtube.com/embed/xyz' — embeds YouTube. Use sandbox attribute to restrict permissions
topics.required_code: Jump to Section 2 About Page Download PDF Google
common_mistakes: Missing alt attribute on img — fails accessibility audit, bad for SEO
Using target='_blank' without rel='noopener noreferrer' — security vulnerability (tab-nabbing attack)

topics.name: HTML Forms — Complete Guide
subtopics: Form basics — action, method, GET vs POST
Input types — text, password, email, number, range, date, color, file, radio, checkbox
Form elements — select, datalist, textarea, meter, progress, label, button
Form validation — required, pattern, minlength, min, max attributes
Regular expressions in pattern attribute
required_content: GET: data in URL (visible, bookmarkable, limited size). POST: data in request body (hidden, no size limit). Use POST for passwords and sensitive data
label for='input-id' — clicking label focuses input. Crucial for accessibility and UX
pattern attribute accepts regex. pattern='[A-Za-z]{3,}' requires 3+ letters. Use with title attribute to show error message
radio buttons must share same name attribute to be mutually exclusive. checkbox each has independent name
topics.required_code:
<!-- Complete login form -->
<form action='/login' method='POST'>
    <label for='email'>Email:</label>
    <input type='email' id='email' name='email' required placeholder='you@example.com'>
    
    <label for='password'>Password:</label>
    <input type='password' id='password' name='password' 
           minlength='8' required
           pattern='(?=.*[A-Z])(?=.*[0-9]).{8,}'
           title='Min 8 chars, one uppercase, one number'>
    
    <label>
        <input type='checkbox' name='remember'> Remember me
    </label>
    
    <button type='submit'>Login</button>
</form>
required_tables: Input types: type | Purpose | Key Attributes || text | Single line text | placeholder, maxlength || password | Hidden text | minlength || email | Email validation | multiple || number | Numeric only | min, max, step || range | Slider | min, max, step || date | Date picker | min, max || file | File upload | accept, multiple || radio | One of many | name (shared) || checkbox | Multiple selection | checked || color | Color picker | value
common_mistakes: Not using label elements — clicking input area is small without label association
Using GET method for login forms — password appears in browser URL history

topics.name: CSS Basics — Selectors and Specificity
subtopics: Three ways to add CSS — inline, embedded (style tag), external stylesheet
CSS selectors — element, class, id, universal (*)
Combinators — descendant, direct child (>), adjacent sibling (+), general sibling (~)
CSS specificity and cascade order
CDN and minification
required_content: Specificity order (lowest to highest): element (0,0,1) → class (0,1,0) → id (1,0,0) → inline (1,0,0,0) → !important
Inline CSS overrides everything but pollutes HTML. External stylesheet is best practice (separation of concerns)
Direct child selector: div > p — only p that are DIRECT children of div. Descendant: div p — any p inside div at any depth
CDN: Content Delivery Network. Bootstrap CDN loads from nearest server worldwide. Faster than local file for common libraries
Minification: removes whitespace and comments from CSS/JS. style.css → style.min.css. Reduces file size by 30-70%
topics.required_code:
/* Selector examples */
* { box-sizing: border-box; }          /* universal */
p { color: #333; }                      /* element */
.card { background: white; }           /* class */
#header { position: fixed; }           /* id */
div > p { margin: 0; }                 /* direct child */
div p { line-height: 1.6; }            /* descendant */
h2 + p { font-size: 1.1rem; }          /* adjacent sibling */
h2 ~ p { color: gray; }                /* general sibling */
required_tables: Specificity: Selector Type | Specificity Value | Example || Inline style | 1,0,0,0 | style='color:red' || ID | 0,1,0,0 | #header || Class | 0,0,1,0 | .card || Attribute | 0,0,1,0 | [type='text'] || Element | 0,0,0,1 | p, div || Universal | 0,0,0,0 | *
common_mistakes: Using id for styling (high specificity makes overriding hard) — use classes for CSS
Thinking last rule always wins — specificity beats order. A class rule later in file won't override an inline style

topics.name: CSS Box Model, Colors and Units
subtopics: CSS Box Model — content, padding, border, margin
box-sizing: content-box vs border-box
CSS Units — absolute (px, pt) vs relative (em, rem, %, vh, vw)
CSS Colors — gradients (linear-gradient, radial-gradient)
box-shadow and border-radius
required_content: Box model layers (inside out): content → padding (inside border) → border → margin (outside border). Total width = content + padding + border
box-sizing: border-box — width includes padding and border (predictable). content-box (default) — width is content only (confusing). Always use border-box
px: fixed pixels. em: relative to parent font-size. rem: relative to root font-size (html). vh/vw: % of viewport height/width
linear-gradient(direction, color1, color2). linear-gradient(to right, #FF6B6B, #4ECDC4) — horizontal gradient
box-shadow: offset-x offset-y blur spread color. box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.1)
required_diagrams: CSS Box Model diagram: concentric rectangles. Outermost = margin (labeled, light blue). Next = border (labeled, dark line). Next = padding (labeled, light green). Center = content (labeled, white). Show measurements on each layer.
common_mistakes: Not setting box-sizing: border-box — elements overflow their containers because padding adds to width
Using px for font sizes — prevents browser accessibility zoom from working. Use rem instead

topics.name: Advanced CSS Selectors and Pseudo-classes
subtopics: Dynamic pseudo-classes — :hover, :focus, :active, :visited, :link
Structural pseudo-selectors — :first-child, :last-child, :nth-child(n), :nth-of-type
Element state pseudo-classes — :enabled, :disabled, :checked, :read-only
Pseudo-elements — ::before, ::after, ::first-line, ::first-letter, ::placeholder
Attribute selectors — [attr], [attr^=], [attr$=], [attr*=]
required_content: :nth-child(2n) selects every even element. :nth-child(2n+1) selects every odd. :nth-child(3n) selects every 3rd
::before and ::after create CSS-only decorative elements. content: '' is required. Used for tooltips, badges, custom bullets
Attribute selectors: [href^='https'] — starts with. [href$='.pdf'] — ends with. [class*='btn'] — contains. Powerful for targeting without extra classes
:focus is critical for keyboard accessibility. Always style focus — never do outline: none without a replacement
common_mistakes: Confusing ::before (pseudo-element, double colon) with :hover (pseudo-class, single colon)
Forgetting content: '' on ::before/::after — element won't appear without content property

modules.module: Positions, Flexbox and Grid in CSS

topics.name: CSS Positions and Display
subtopics: CSS position values — static, relative, absolute, fixed, sticky
z-index — controlling stacking order
Display values — none, block, inline, inline-block, flex, grid
Visibility vs display:none distinction
required_content: static (default): normal flow. relative: offset from normal position, keeps space. absolute: removed from flow, relative to nearest positioned ancestor. fixed: relative to viewport, stays on scroll. sticky: relative until scroll threshold, then fixed
z-index only works on positioned elements (not static). Higher z-index = on top. Negative values go behind
block: full width, new line (div, p, h1). inline: content width, same line (span, a, img). inline-block: content width but accepts width/height
display:none — removes from layout, invisible. visibility:hidden — invisible but keeps space. opacity:0 — invisible, keeps space, still clickable
required_diagrams: CSS position comparison: 4 small diagrams side by side. 1) relative — element in flow, shifted with dotted outline showing original position. 2) absolute — element lifted out of flow, positioned in corner of parent. 3) fixed — element pinned to viewport corner, page scrolling behind it. 4) sticky — element scrolling normally, then freezing at top.
common_mistakes: Using position:absolute without setting position:relative on parent — element positions relative to body/html instead
Setting z-index on static elements — has no effect, must set position first

topics.name: Flexbox — Complete Guide
subtopics: Flex container properties — display:flex, flex-direction, flex-wrap, justify-content, align-items
Flex item properties — flex-grow, flex-shrink, flex-basis, align-self, order
Main axis vs cross axis concept
Common Flexbox layouts — navbar, card row, centered content
required_content: flex-direction: row (default, horizontal) or column (vertical). This defines the MAIN AXIS. Cross axis is perpendicular
justify-content: aligns items along MAIN AXIS. Values: flex-start, flex-end, center, space-between, space-around, space-evenly
align-items: aligns items along CROSS AXIS. Values: flex-start, flex-end, center, stretch (default), baseline
flex: 1 — shorthand for flex-grow:1, flex-shrink:1, flex-basis:0. Makes item take equal share of available space
required_diagrams: Flexbox axes diagram: show a flex container with row direction. Label horizontal line as MAIN AXIS with justify-content. Label vertical line as CROSS AXIS with align-items. Show items positioned by space-between on main axis and center on cross axis.
common_mistakes: Applying flex properties to the wrong element — display:flex goes on PARENT (container), not on the items themselves
Confusing justify-content (main axis) with align-items (cross axis) — swaps when flex-direction changes to column

topics.name: CSS Grid — Complete Guide
subtopics: Grid container — display:grid, grid-template-columns, grid-template-rows, grid-gap
The fr unit and repeat() function
Grid item placement — grid-column, grid-row, grid-area
auto-fill vs auto-fit for responsive grids
required_content: grid-template-columns: 200px 1fr 1fr — first column 200px fixed, other two share remaining space equally
repeat(3, 1fr) = 1fr 1fr 1fr. repeat(auto-fill, minmax(250px, 1fr)) — creates as many 250px+ columns as fit
grid-column: 1 / 3 — item spans from column line 1 to line 3 (occupies 2 columns). grid-column: 1 / -1 — spans all columns
Grid vs Flexbox: Grid = 2D layout (rows AND columns). Flexbox = 1D layout (row OR column). Use Grid for page layout, Flexbox for component layout
required_diagrams: CSS Grid visual: show a 3x3 grid with lines numbered 1-4 on both axes. Highlight one item spanning columns 1-3 (grid-column: 1/3). Show gap between cells. Label grid lines, tracks, cells, and areas.
common_mistakes: Confusing grid lines (numbers) with column counts — grid-column: 1/3 spans 2 columns (from line 1 to line 3)
Using Grid for simple single-row layouts — Flexbox is simpler and more appropriate

topics.name: CSS Typography, Transforms and Transitions
subtopics: CSS Font properties — font-family, font-size, font-weight, font-style
Text properties — text-align, text-decoration, text-transform, line-height, letter-spacing
CSS 2D Transforms — translate, rotate, scale, skew
CSS Transitions — property, duration, timing-function, delay
required_content: font-family fallback stack: font-family: 'Roboto', Arial, sans-serif — browser tries each in order. Always end with generic family (serif, sans-serif, monospace)
line-height: 1.6 (unitless, recommended) — 1.6x the font size. Better than px because scales with font
transform: translate(x, y) — moves element without affecting layout flow. translate(-50%, -50%) used for perfect centering
transition: property duration timing-function delay. transition: all 0.3s ease — smooth animation on any property change
common_mistakes: Using margin-top to push elements for visual effects — use transform:translateY for animations, it doesn't cause reflow
Setting transition: all — can cause performance issues, specify exact properties instead

topics.name: CSS Animations and Media Queries
subtopics: CSS Animations — @keyframes, animation properties
CSS Filter functions — blur, brightness, contrast, grayscale
Media Queries — min-width, max-width, orientation
Responsive design — mobile-first approach, fluid images, viewport meta
required_content: @keyframes defines animation states. animation: name duration timing-function iteration. animation: spin 2s linear infinite
Mobile-first: write CSS for mobile first, then add media queries for larger screens. min-width queries build UP from small
Standard breakpoints: 576px (sm), 768px (md), 992px (lg), 1200px (xl)
Fluid images: img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; } — image never overflows container but shrinks on small screens
common_mistakes: Desktop-first media queries (max-width) — harder to maintain, requires overriding more styles. Use mobile-first (min-width)
Animating width/height — triggers layout reflow, causes jank. Animate transform and opacity instead

topics.name: JavaScript Fundamentals — Variables, Data Types, Operators
subtopics: Introduction to JS — AOT vs JIT compilation, JS engines (V8)
Variables — var, let, const differences, scopes
Data types — primitive (string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol) vs non-primitive
Operators — arithmetic, comparison, logical, ternary, nullish coalescing (??)
required_content: var: function-scoped, hoisted, can redeclare. let: block-scoped, not hoisted (TDZ), can reassign. const: block-scoped, must initialize, cannot reassign (but object properties CAN change)
typeof null === 'object' — famous JS bug. typeof undefined === 'undefined'. typeof function === 'function'
== (loose equality) does type coercion: '5' == 5 is true. === (strict) checks type AND value: '5' === 5 is false. Always use ===
?? (nullish coalescing): returns right side only if left is null or undefined. Different from || which triggers on any falsy value (0, '', false)
required_tables: var vs let vs const: Feature | var | let | const || Scope | Function | Block | Block || Hoisting | Yes (undefined) | No (TDZ) | No (TDZ) || Redeclaration | Allowed | Error | Error || Reassignment | Allowed | Allowed | Error || Use today | Avoid | Yes | Preferred
common_mistakes: Using == instead of === — '0' == false is true in JS, causes subtle bugs
Thinking const prevents mutation — const prevents reassignment of the variable, NOT mutation of the object it points to

topics.name: DOM Manipulation and Events
subtopics: Targeting DOM elements — getElementById, querySelector, querySelectorAll, getElementsByClassName
Reading and modifying DOM — innerHTML, innerText, textContent, value
Modifying CSS via JS — style property, classList (add, remove, toggle)
Events — addEventListener, event object, common events (click, input, change, submit, keydown)
required_content: querySelector returns FIRST match (or null). querySelectorAll returns NodeList of ALL matches. NodeList is NOT an array (use Array.from() or spread)
innerHTML parses HTML (XSS risk with user input). innerText shows only visible text. textContent shows all text including hidden
classList.toggle('active') adds class if absent, removes if present. classList.contains('active') returns boolean
addEventListener('click', handler) preferred over onclick attribute — can add multiple handlers, can remove them
common_mistakes: Running DOM code before page loads — wrap in DOMContentLoaded or put script at bottom of body
Using innerHTML with user input without sanitization — XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) vulnerability

topics.name: Functions, Scope and Closures
subtopics: Function declarations vs expressions vs arrow functions
Scope — global, function, block scope
Hoisting — variable and function hoisting
Closures — function remembering its outer scope
Callbacks and higher-order functions
required_content: Arrow functions: shorter syntax, no own 'this'. const add = (a, b) => a + b. NOT suitable as methods or constructors
Function declarations are hoisted completely (can call before declaration). Function expressions are NOT hoisted
Closure: inner function retains access to outer function's variables even after outer function returns. Used for data privacy and factory functions
Higher-order functions: functions that take/return functions. Array methods (map, filter, reduce) are HOFs
common_mistakes: Using arrow functions as object methods — 'this' refers to outer scope, not the object
Confusing closure with global variables — closure captures variables from the specific outer function call, not global

modules.module: Conditionals, Loops and Arrays in JavaScript

topics.name: Conditionals and Control Flow
subtopics: if, else if, else — simple and nested conditions
Switch statement — when to use over if-else
Ternary operator — single-line conditional
Short-circuit evaluation — && and || for conditional execution
required_content: switch uses strict equality (===). Don't forget break — without it, execution falls through to next case. Use default as last case
Ternary: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse. Good for simple cases, not for complex logic (hurts readability)
Short-circuit: user && user.name — if user is falsy, stops and returns user (prevents error). isLoggedIn || redirectToLogin() — runs redirectToLogin only if not logged in
Truthy/Falsy: falsy values in JS: false, 0, '', null, undefined, NaN. Everything else is truthy
common_mistakes: Missing break in switch — code falls through to next case unintentionally
Using || for default values when 0 or '' are valid — use ?? (nullish coalescing) instead

topics.name: Loops and Iteration
subtopics: for loop — classic counter-based loop
while and do-while — condition-based loops
for...in — iterating object keys
for...of — iterating iterables (arrays, strings, NodeList)
break, continue, return — loop control
required_content: for...in iterates over KEYS of an object (or array indices). for...of iterates over VALUES of an iterable. Use for...of for arrays, for...in for objects
do-while executes body AT LEAST ONCE before checking condition. Use when you need to run first, then check
break exits the loop entirely. continue skips to next iteration. return exits the entire function
Array forEach is cleaner for arrays: arr.forEach((item, index) => ...). Cannot use break/continue inside forEach
common_mistakes: Using for...in on arrays — iterates indices as strings, also iterates prototype properties. Use for...of for arrays
Modifying array length while looping — causes skipped/repeated elements. Create new array with filter/map instead

topics.name: Arrays and Array Methods
subtopics: Array basics — creation, indexing, push, pop, shift, unshift, splice, slice
Searching — indexOf, find, findIndex, includes, some, every
Transformation — map, filter, reduce
Spread operator and array destructuring
Exception handling — try, catch, throw, finally
required_content: map returns NEW array of same length (transforms each item). filter returns NEW array of subset (removes items). reduce returns single value (accumulates)
slice(start, end) — non-destructive, returns copy. splice(start, deleteCount, ...items) — destructive, modifies original, returns removed items
find returns FIRST matching element (or undefined). filter returns ALL matching elements (as array)
Spread: [...arr1, ...arr2] combines arrays. [...arr] creates shallow copy. Destructuring: const [first, ...rest] = arr
common_mistakes: Using splice when you mean slice — splice mutates the original array, slice does not
Expecting map/filter to mutate original array — they return NEW arrays, original is unchanged
